metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete module path for the new `related_ticket_ids` helper (e.g. `src/rebar/graph/relations.py`) so the executor doesn't have to invent the location.
findings.finding: The location of the new shared `related_ticket_ids` helper is under-specified: 'co-located with the extracted parent-map util' defers the file-placement decision to the executor because the extraction destination is not yet decided.
findings.location: Scope section — 'new shared related_ticket_ids helper (co-located with the extracted parent-map util)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'New `src/rebar/graph/relations.py` exporting `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` and the extracted `build_parent_map` util; both `next_batch.py` (refactored) and `retrieve.py` import from this module.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting (a) the decision to extract `related_ticket_ids` as a shared util and its interface contract, and (b) the S7 interface (`retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude) -> list[OverlapCandidate]`) with its stability guarantees, so future contributors have a canonical record.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new pipeline stage (`src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py`) and a new cross-component shared helper (`related_ticket_ids`) extracted from `next_batch.py`, but does not include any update to an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design document. For a novel pipeline stage that establishes a new inter-component contract (the S7 interface) and a new shared utility refactored out of private BFS logic, the absence of a durable architectural record leaves future maintainers without context for why the extraction was done and what the interface guarantees.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New `src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py`; new shared `related_ticket_ids` helper; `overlap` config weights/knobs'
findings.suggested_fix: A single CLAUDE.md block or `docs/adr/0NNN-overlap-stage1.md` covering: the extraction rationale, the `related_ticket_ids` interface contract (inputs, outputs, what 'related' means), and the S7 retrieve interface. Minimal prose (10–20 lines) is sufficient.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit file path for the `related_ticket_ids` shared helper (e.g. `src/rebar/_engine_support/graph_util.py` or `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py`) in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan does not name an exact file path for the new shared `related_ticket_ids` helper — it says 'co-located with the extracted parent-map util' but the 'extracted parent-map util' file path is itself unspecified. This leaves two edit targets (the helper module and its colocation file) without concrete paths.
findings.location: What section — 'new small shared helper related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str] (co-located with the extracted parent-map util)'; Scope section — 'new shared related_ticket_ids helper (co-located with the extracted parent-map util)'
findings.suggested_fix: Given that descendants.py already lives in src/rebar/_engine_support/ and performs closely related graph traversal, co-locate the helper there (e.g. add related_ticket_ids to descendants.py or a new src/rebar/_engine_support/graph.py) and name that path explicitly in Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific library providing BM25F (or explicitly state 'bespoke implementation') and, if bespoke, reference the BM25F formula (Zaragoza et al.) including per-field TF normalisation and weight-folding so implementers cannot silently substitute standard BM25.
findings.finding: The plan asserts BM25F (the field-weighted variant of BM25) as the scoring algorithm but names no Python library providing it and lists no dependency to add. No BM25 library of any kind exists in pyproject.toml. The popular `rank_bm25` library provides BM25Okapi/BM25L/BM25Plus — not BM25F (which requires per-field IDF and length normalisation). If the intent is a bespoke implementation, the formula complexity (per-field TF with separate length normalisations, per-field weights folded before IDF) is non-trivial and must be explicitly validated; if an existing library is intended, it must be named and verified to support BM25F specifically.
findings.location: What section — 'A brute-force BM25F "More Like This" scorer'; Scope — 'New src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py (BM25F + candidate type)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'BM25F scorer is a bespoke pure-Python implementation of the Zaragoza et al. (2004) formula — per-field term frequency with independent length normalisation (b_f per field), weight-folded before IDF (no external library dependency; add `rank_bm25` only if BM25F support is confirmed). Alternatively, add `rank_bm25` to pyproject.toml `[agents]` extra and verify its BM25F support before committing to it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `overlap` typed config key vocabulary: name every key (`k`, `max_doc_freq`, `stopwords`, `min_overlap`, and each field weight key), its type (int/float/list[str]/dict), and its default value, co-located with the `OverlapCandidate` schema description in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The `overlap` typed config section's key vocabulary — the specific key names, their types, and their default values — is never defined. The plan says weights, `max_doc_freq`, stopword list, `minimum_should_match`-style floor, and `K` are 'configurable via the `overlap` typed config' but names no key. An executor must invent all key identifiers (e.g., `overlap.k` vs `overlap.top_k`; `overlap.field_weights.problem_keywords` vs `overlap.problem_keywords_weight`) and the AC's `::test_knobs` cannot be written without them.
findings.location: What section (configurable knobs) + Scope section ('`overlap` config weights/knobs in `rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])`') + AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a config table to the Scope section, e.g.: `overlap.k` (int, default 20), `overlap.max_doc_freq` (float 0–1, default 0.3), `overlap.stopwords` (list[str], default []), `overlap.min_overlap` (int, default 1), `overlap.weights.problem_keywords` (float, default 2.0), `overlap.weights.component_or_area` (float, default 2.0), `overlap.weights.propositions` (float, default 1.0), `overlap.weights.key_entities` (float, default 1.0). Confirm these are added as fields on a new dataclass (e.g., `OverlapConfig`) resolved within the `rebar.llm` layer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py`'s `list_descendants()` can satisfy (or be extended to satisfy) the `related_ticket_ids` use-case before directing creation of a new shared BFS util — the plan's assertion that no reusable shared util exists is contradicted by this file.
findings.finding: The plan directs creating a NEW shared `related_ticket_ids` helper by factoring the parent-map/BFS logic out of `next_batch.py`, asserting no reusable shared util exists. But `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py` already exports a public `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)` function that performs exactly this generic BFS (builds `children_of` from `reduce_all_tickets`, walks from any root). The plan's factoring directive and the implied absence of a reusable util are false — the existing module could be reused or extended instead of a new one created.
findings.location: What section — 'Graph-exclusion filter' paragraph: 'factor the parent-map/BFS logic — next_batch.py's BFS is epic-scoped, private, and NOT reusable as-is; factor the parent-map build into a shared util used by both'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 'factor the parent-map build into a shared util' directive with: 'Implement `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker) -> set[str]` in `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py` (or as a thin wrapper over it), reusing the existing generic BFS infrastructure in that module rather than duplicating it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py::list_descendants` (already a public BFS graph-walker) can serve as the basis for `related_ticket_ids` before factoring new logic out of `next_batch.py`; document the decision.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new shared `related_ticket_ids` helper backed by factoring parent-map/BFS logic out of `next_batch.py`, but `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py` already exports a public `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)` function implementing exactly a BFS parent→child walk — prior art the plan neither considers nor rules out.
findings.location: What section — 'new small shared helper `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` (extract the parent-map/BFS logic — `next_batch.py`'s BFS is epic-scoped, private, and NOT reusable as-is; factor the parent-map build into a shared util used by both)'
findings.suggested_fix: Inspect descendants.list_descendants — it returns all descendant IDs of a root. For `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id)`, composing parent lookup + descendants.list_descendants + sibling enumeration over the existing children_of map may suffice without any new BFS implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to a bespoke BM25F implementation, audit whether an existing Python library (Whoosh BM25F, rank_bm25 with field-weighted concatenation, bm25s) satisfies the field-weighted scoring requirement; document the decision (use it or explain why it was ruled out) in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan commits to a bespoke 'BM25F' implementation (a field-weighted variant, not standard BM25) without naming any library that provides it, without checking whether existing Python BM25 libraries (rank_bm25, bm25s, Whoosh, Elasticsearch More-Like-This) actually implement BM25F field-weighting, and without ruling out the use of an off-the-shelf solution — a direct unverified-capability-assertion + prior-art gap.
findings.location: What section — 'A brute-force BM25F "More Like This" scorer'; Scope — 'New src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py (BM25F + candidate type)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dependency evaluation section: check Whoosh (has native BM25F), rank_bm25 (BM25 variants only — field weighting via pre-normalized concatenation is a known workaround), and bm25s (fast but no field weighting). If none fit cleanly, state that explicitly and describe the bespoke BM25F formula to be implemented (Robertson et al. field-normalization constants B_f, K1).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_engine_support/next_batch.py` to the Scope's edit-set and enumerate its callers (`_reads.py`, `_lib_reads.py`, `_mcp_reads.py`, `reads_cli.py`) as consumers requiring regression-test verification after the parent-map extraction.
findings.finding: next_batch.py is an implicit edit target (the plan says 'factor the parent-map build into a shared util used by both'), but the plan's Scope section does not enumerate next_batch.py as an edited file, and its external callers (src/rebar/_reads.py, src/rebar/_lib_reads.py, src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py, src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py) are unenumerated consumers that could be broken by internal restructuring of that module.
findings.location: Scope section (lists only retrieve.py, shared helper, overlap config); What section — 'factor the parent-map build into a shared util used by both'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py` (public BFS/children_of) as the primary reuse candidate for the shared graph helper, and either reuse it directly or explicitly justify why a new extraction is needed instead.
findings.finding: The plan proposes factoring the parent-map/BFS logic from next_batch.py into a new shared util, but src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py already exports a public list_descendants() BFS (children_of map, deque walk) that is structurally identical. The edit-set is inaccurate: the plan creates new code that duplicates an existing reusable symbol rather than referencing it, and does not acknowledge descendants.py as an alternative edit target.
findings.location: What section — 'factor the parent-map build into a shared util used by both'; src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'factor the parent-map build into a shared util' with 'extend or wrap src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py#list_descendants to accept a pre-loaded all_states dict, so related_ticket_ids can call it without re-loading the full corpus.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact mechanism for the 'overlap typed config': name the new dataclass (e.g. `OverlapConfig`), where it lives (a field on `LLMConfig` or a standalone dataclass in `overlap/retrieve.py`), and how it is populated from `[tool.rebar.llm]` (a new `_llm_*` resolver call, a nested sub-table, or a separate `read_reserved_section('llm.overlap')` call).
findings.finding: The plan places overlap BM25F configuration in 'the overlap typed config' within 'rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])' — but no `overlap` sub-config field or dataclass exists on LLMConfig, and [tool.rebar.llm] is a flat dict with specific known keys parsed by _read_llm_file_table(). The named edit target ('overlap typed config') is a hallucinated symbol; the plan does not specify what new field/class/key is added to LLMConfig or config.py.
findings.location: Scope section — 'overlap config weights/knobs in rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `OverlapConfig` dataclass to `src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py` with typed fields for weights, max_doc_freq, stopwords, overlap_floor, and K; instantiate it from plain kwargs so callers can pass it directly; read it from `[tool.rebar.llm]` flat keys (e.g. `overlap_k`, `overlap_max_doc_freq`) via the existing `_llm_int`/`_llm_str` helpers in `LLMConfig.from_env`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC item (and corresponding test assertion or existing-test reference) confirming that `next_batch.py`'s behavior is preserved after the parent-map logic is extracted into the shared util.
findings.finding: The plan extracts parent-map logic from `next_batch.py` into a shared util and changes `next_batch.py` to use it, but does not acknowledge `next_batch.py`'s existing callers or regression-test coverage. If `next_batch.py` has callers or unit tests exercising its BFS/parent-map path, the refactor could silently break them, and no AC or test in this plan guards against that.
findings.location: Scope section — 'new shared `related_ticket_ids` helper (co-located with the extracted parent-map util)'; What section — 'factor the parent-map build into a shared util used by both'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Testing: 'Existing `next_batch.py` tests must remain green post-refactor (run `pytest tests/unit/test_next_batch.py` as part of this stage's CI gate).' If no such tests exist, note them as a prerequisite.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `minimum_should_match`-style floor's concrete semantics in the What section: state whether it is an integer (minimum count of distinct query terms matched), a float (fraction of query terms), and what happens when the query has zero terms after stopword filtering.
findings.finding: The `minimum_should_match`-style overlap floor's semantics are never defined. The qualifier 'style' signals it is not the literal Elasticsearch `minimum_should_match`, but the plan never specifies whether the floor is an integer (minimum unique matching terms), a float fraction (fraction of query terms that must match), or a percentage string. An executor cannot implement or test this without choosing semantics.
findings.location: What section: 'a `minimum_should_match`-style overlap floor'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Error handling sub-section whether 'scoring exception' means a whole-retrieve abort (returning []) or a per-ticket skip (that ticket is omitted and counted with the stale-digest skips), and add a corresponding test case to distinguish the two failure modes.
findings.finding: The fallback for a per-ticket scoring exception (as opposed to a whole-graph-read or whole-corpus exception) is unspecified. The plan says 'a graph-read or scoring exception is caught, logged, and yields an empty candidate list', but it is ambiguous whether this means (a) any exception anywhere returns [], or (b) a per-ticket scoring error causes that one ticket to be skipped while scoring continues. These two behaviors are observably different and affect the semantics of the skip-count log.
findings.location: What section: 'Error handling: a graph-read or scoring exception is caught, logged, and yields an empty candidate list'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add named test functions for AC5's two sub-claims (e.g. `::test_configurable_knobs` asserting K=5 returns exactly 5 candidates, and `::test_error_yields_empty` asserting a graph-read exception returns `[]`), and cite them in AC5.
findings.finding: AC5 names no proving command or test function for either sub-claim ('configurable via typed config' and 'error yields empty list'), leaving both unverifiable by a concrete pytest reference.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'Field weights, max_doc_freq, overlap floor, and K are configurable via the overlap typed config; a graph-read/scoring error yields an empty list (never blocks the caller).'
findings.suggested_fix: AC5 revised: '…; verified by `::test_configurable_knobs` (override K=5 → assert len(result)==5, override weight → assert score order changes) and `::test_error_yields_empty` (mock graph read raises RuntimeError → assert result==[]).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit `verified by ::test_configurable_knobs` and `verified by ::test_error_yields_empty` (or equivalent named tests) to AC5 to match the verification pattern of ACs 1–4.
findings.finding: AC5 lacks a `verified by` test reference — every other AC names specific test functions (e.g. `::test_topk`, `::test_perf`) but AC5 omits this, leaving configurability and error-handling behavior unverifiably specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (last bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC5 into two ACs or append: 'verified by `::test_configurable_knobs, ::test_error_yields_empty`' to the existing AC5 bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `next_batch.py` parent-map refactor — e.g. an expand-contract sequence (add shared util first, migrate next_batch.py second under a passing test gate, remove old code only after CI green) or a stated fallback if extraction regresses.
findings.finding: The plan refactors `next_batch.py`'s parent-map/BFS logic into a shared util (modifying existing, in-use code) but provides no rollback path or back-out plan if the refactor introduces a regression.
findings.location: What section — 'factor the parent-map build into a shared util used by both'; Scope — 'new shared `related_ticket_ids` helper (co-located with the extracted parent-map util)'
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt an expand-contract sequence: (1) add `related_ticket_ids` shared util alongside the existing next_batch.py logic, (2) add tests that assert behavioral equivalence, (3) migrate next_batch.py to use the shared util, (4) delete the old inline logic only after CI green. State this sequence explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing a new shared parent-map/BFS util, confirm whether `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py`'s `list_descendants` (or its internal `children_of` map) can be called directly to enumerate parents, children, and siblings — only build a new util if `descendants.py` is genuinely insufficient for the overlap use-case.
findings.finding: The plan proposes factoring 'parent-map/BFS logic from `next_batch.py` into a new shared util,' but `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py` already exports a public, production `list_descendants(root_id, tracker) -> dict` function that builds a `children_of` map and runs BFS from any root ticket — exactly the graph-traversal needed for parent/child/sibling detection. Building a new shared util here is NIH against existing production code.
findings.location: What section: 'factor the parent-map build into a shared util used by both'; Scope: 'new shared `related_ticket_ids` helper (co-located with the extracted parent-map util)'
findings.suggested_fix: Call `list_descendants(query_ticket_id, tracker)` from `descendants.py` to get children/descendants of the query ticket's parent; for sibling detection, call it on the query ticket's `parent_id`. This avoids creating a new shared util entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a tokenization/normalization protocol to the What section specifying at minimum: case normalization (lower), character classes split on (whitespace? punctuation?), and whether stopword filtering applies pre- or post-splitting — so `matched_terms` in `OverlapCandidate` has a deterministic vocabulary.
findings.finding: The tokenization/normalization protocol for BM25F term extraction is referenced implicitly (stopword filtering, `max_doc_freq`) but never co-located with the BM25F scorer schema. An executor must invent whether field values are lowercased, split on whitespace only, punctuation-stripped, or stemmed — decisions that determine which terms appear in `matched_terms` and whether the boilerplate test (`::test_boilerplate`) can pass deterministically.
findings.location: What section (BM25F scorer description + stopword/max_doc_freq knobs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State numeric defaults for all configurable knobs (field weights for each digest field, overlap floor value, max_doc_freq threshold) in the plan so the executor can implement without inventing values.
findings.finding: No numeric defaults are stated for field weights (described only as 'high'/'lower') or the minimum_should_match overlap floor, forcing the executor to invent values rather than implement a stated default.
findings.location: What section — 'weights configurable', 'minimum_should_match-style overlap floor'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or inline defaults, e.g.: `problem_keywords` weight=2.0, `component_or_area` weight=2.0, `propositions`/`key_entities` weight=1.0; overlap floor=1 matched term; max_doc_freq=0.7 (fraction of corpus); K=20.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe the configurability AC as a behavioral observable: e.g. 'overriding K=5 in config returns exactly 5 candidates; overriding a field weight changes result ordering — verified by ::test_configurable_knobs.'
findings.finding: AC5's 'configurable via typed config' sub-claim is effort-framed rather than outcome-observable — it states a structural property (config is typed) not a behavioral outcome (changing a knob changes scorer behavior), making it unmeasurable in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5
findings.suggested_fix: 'Given default K=20 and corpus of 50 digests, result length == 20; given K=5, result length == 5. Given problem_keywords weight=0.0, a query matching only that field returns score 0. Verified by ::test_configurable_knobs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the 'What' or 'Scope' section explaining why BM25F (with per-field IDF/avgfl) was chosen — e.g. that field-weighting on structured digest documents requires true BM25F (not field-concatenation BM25 or TF-IDF cosine), and why a bespoke implementation is preferred over rank_bm25/bm25s (e.g. no library supports BM25F with per-field normalization out of the box at this corpus size).
findings.finding: The plan commits to a bespoke pure-Python BM25F implementation but states no positive rationale for why BM25F was chosen over simpler alternatives (e.g. TF-IDF cosine, basic BM25 via rank_bm25/bm25s) or why a from-scratch implementation is preferred over an existing library. The absence of this rationale leaves the implementer without documented justification and makes the choice appear arbitrary, which can lead to under-constrained implementation (e.g. implementing concatenated-field BM25 instead of true BM25F with per-field avgfl).
findings.location: ## What — 'A brute-force BM25F "More Like This" scorer'; ## Scope — 'New src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py (BM25F + candidate type)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, after the BM25F scorer description, add: 'BM25F is chosen over basic BM25 or TF-IDF cosine because field-weighted scoring over structurally distinct digest fields (keywords vs. propositions vs. area) requires per-field length normalization and boosts — a property BM25F provides natively. A bespoke implementation is used because existing Python BM25 libraries (rank_bm25, bm25s) implement BM25Okapi/BM25L over flat token bags, not true BM25F with per-field avgfl, making field-boost semantics unavailable without significant wrapping.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly confirm that all `overlap` BM25F tunables are routed through a new `overlap` sub-table of `[tool.rebar.llm]` (i.e., `[tool.rebar.llm.overlap]`) read via the existing `_read_llm_file_table` / reserved-section mechanism — NOT via a new entry in `_SECTIONS` in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan places BM25F tuning knobs (`field weights`, `max_doc_freq`, `stopword list`, `overlap floor`, `K`) in 'the `overlap` typed config in `rebar.llm` config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])'. The `_config_schema.py` `_SECTIONS` dict does not include an `overlap` entry, and its architecture explicitly reserves the `llm` key for the `[agents]` extra, not core sections. Adding an `[overlap]` section to `_SECTIONS` (core) would violate this separation; routing it through `[tool.rebar.llm]`'s raw table (the correct approach) is not confirmed by the plan.
findings.location: Scope: 'overlap config weights/knobs in rebar.llm config layer (LLMConfig / [tool.rebar.llm])'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `OverlapConfig` dataclass in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` (alongside `LangfuseConfig`) populated from the `table.get('overlap', {})` sub-dict within `_read_llm_file_table`; attach it as a field on `LLMConfig`. This keeps BM25F tunables inside the `[tool.rebar.llm]` reserved section and out of the stdlib core schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the ambiguous 'reduced ticket state / `ticket_deps` data' phrase with a precise reference to the actual read surface (e.g. `reduce_all_tickets` / `reduce_ticket` from `rebar.reducer`), since `ticket_deps` in the codebase names the MCP tool, not a data layer.
findings.finding: The plan states graph-exclusion 'Reads go through the reduced ticket state / `ticket_deps` data, not the MCP tool.' In the codebase, `ticket_deps` is exclusively an MCP tool function (`src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py:117`), not a data store or module. The underlying data the plan actually intends comes from `reduce_all_tickets` / `reduce_ticket`. Using the name `ticket_deps` to refer to the data source is a false-by-labeling assertion that could lead implementers to look for a non-existent `ticket_deps` data layer or mistakenly invoke the MCP surface.
findings.location: What section — 'Graph-exclusion filter' paragraph: 'Reads go through the reduced ticket state / `ticket_deps` data, not the MCP tool.'
findings.suggested_fix: Change to: 'Reads go through `reduce_all_tickets` / `reduce_ticket` (from `rebar.reducer`) directly — not the MCP `ticket_deps` tool.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a calibration spike (even a one-off script) that runs the BM25F scorer with candidate default weights over a sample of real `ticket_digest` records and reports top-K overlap vs. a manually-labelled ground truth, so the shipped defaults are data-derived rather than asserted.
findings.finding: Default field weights (problem_keywords/component_or_area = high; propositions/key_entities = lower) are asserted by the author's judgment rather than derived from any calibration run on real ticket data. The unit tests exercise only synthetic fixtures. If the defaults are poorly tuned, Stage 1 will silently under-recall on real corpora, and the miscalibration will only surface after the full pipeline is wired.
findings.location: What section — 'Field-weighted: `problem_keywords` and `component_or_area` high, `propositions`/`key_entities` lower (weights configurable)'
findings.suggested_fix: Include a `scripts/calibrate_overlap_weights.py` (or a one-off notebook) that: (1) loads 20–50 real digest pairs with known relationships, (2) scores them under candidate weight vectors, (3) reports recall@20, and (4) records the chosen weights and the recall figure in a comment in the config schema. This need not block the unit tests but should gate the weight defaults before the plan is marked done.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `ticket_deps` reads in `related_ticket_ids` are snapshot-isolated (e.g., a single atomic read at the start of `retrieve`) or document that callers must pass a pre-fetched, frozen exclusion set to avoid TOCTOU races.
findings.finding: The `related_ticket_ids` helper reads `ticket_deps` data at call time with no mention of snapshot isolation or a point-in-time read guarantee. If another process concurrently updates `ticket_deps` (e.g., a parent link is added mid-review-run), the exclusion set could be inconsistently computed — some calls see the new link, others don't — leading to a TOCTOU-style race where a ticket that should be excluded slips through.
findings.location: What section — 'Reads go through the reduced ticket state / ticket_deps data, not the MCP tool'; T9 checklist item: 'Shared/mutable state mutated atomically … no check-then-act TOCTOU'
findings.suggested_fix: Resolve the exclusion set once at the start of `retrieve` (before scoring begins) by calling `related_ticket_ids` a single time and caching its result in a local `frozenset`; document this as the concurrency contract in the function's docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `related_ticket_ids` is scoped as an `overlap`-internal function (not a shared util) unless a second concrete call-site is identified in this stage or an immediately upcoming stage; a private function in `retrieve.py` satisfies the done-definition with no premature abstraction.
findings.finding: The proposed `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` helper has exactly one stated call-site (the graph-exclusion filter in `retrieve.py`). The Rule-of-Three requires ≥3 existing call-sites before extracting a shared util; extracting it for a single consumer is premature abstraction.
findings.location: What section: 'new small shared helper `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Make `related_ticket_ids` a module-private function in `src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py`; only extract it to a shared util when a second concrete consumer is identified (Rule-of-Three: extract on the third, not the first).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
